## Memo: Build vs. Buy — Internal Analytics Dashboard

**To:** Leadership / Eng / Data
**Date:** 2026-06-22
**Decision owner:** [name]
**Recommendation:** Buy (adopt an off-the-shelf BI tool) for the core dashboard now; reserve custom build only for the one or two metrics that are genuinely differentiating and not well-served by the tool.

### The decision
We need an internal analytics dashboard for [revenue, funnel, retention, ops health]. The question is whether to build it ourselves or stand up a vendor BI tool (e.g. Metabase, Looker Studio, Superset, Mode, Hex). This memo recommends a default and lists the few facts that would flip it.

### Why "buy" is the default
An internal dashboard is a solved problem and not a source of competitive advantage. The real cost of building is not v1 — it's the permanent maintenance tax: auth, access control, chart rendering, query caching, scheduled refreshes, and the on-call burden when a panel breaks at 9am before a board meeting. A vendor absorbs all of that. For most teams, time-to-first-insight with a bought tool is days; a built one is weeks to months, plus ongoing eng headcount.

### When building is actually right (flip conditions)
Choose build only if **two or more** hold:
- Your core metrics require logic no BI tool models cleanly (complex attribution, bespoke cohorting) **and** that logic is itself part of the product's edge.
- Data residency / compliance forbids piping data to a third-party SaaS, and self-hosting a vendor tool is also ruled out.
- You need the dashboard embedded in a customer-facing product (different decision — that's a product feature, not internal tooling).
- Vendor per-seat cost at your projected user count exceeds the fully-loaded cost of the eng time to build and maintain (run the actual number; it rarely does for internal-only use).

### Recommended path
1. **Buy the platform, own the data layer.** Keep transformations in your warehouse / dbt-style models that you control; let the vendor handle only visualization and access. This avoids lock-in — your metric definitions stay portable.
2. **Self-host if compliance needs it.** Metabase or Superset self-hosted gives you control of the data path without building rendering and auth yourself.
3. **Build narrowly, later.** If one differentiating view genuinely can't be expressed in the tool, build that single view against the same warehouse — not the whole dashboard.

### Rough cost comparison (fill with real numbers)
| | Buy (SaaS/self-host BI) | Build in-house |
|---|---|---|
| Time to v1 | Days–2 weeks | 1–3+ months |
| Upfront cost | License / hosting | Eng salary × duration |
| Ongoing | Subscription + light admin | Permanent maintenance + on-call |
| Risk | Vendor lock-in, per-seat scaling | Bus factor, scope creep, neglect |

### Next steps
- [ ] Pick 2 vendor candidates and run a 1-week trial against real warehouse data.
- [ ] Price both at projected seat count vs. one eng-month build estimate.
- [ ] Confirm the compliance/data-residency constraint (this is the most common dealbreaker — settle it first).
- [ ] Decide by [date].

---

I wrote this with placeholder assumptions (metrics, vendor shortlist, seat count) since I don't have your specifics — the bracketed fields and the cost table are meant to be filled in. If you tell me your team size, the metrics you actually need, and any data-residency constraints, I'll tighten this into a final version with a concrete recommendation and real numbers, and can save it to your documents folder if you want.
